P.S. I started with TinkerCAD and progressed onto using 123Design. Worth having a play with, as it will feel pretty familiar if you are used to TinerCAD, but it has more features. I think they are no longer updating it though, but it can still be downloaded I believe. Plenty of Youtube tutorials on it too.

text: I had to learn gear design because I simply couldn't find anyone to hire to build gears for me. It's not an educational requirement for modern machinist to know gear design, and most who do know gear design are working in the aerospace field. Gear design is simple, but if you don't brush up on it every once in a while, you'll lose it "from my experience".

replies.author: @howardosborne8647
replies.text: in simple terms without taking any mechanical losses into account a 2:1 reduction ratio will double the torque from the input side....10lb/ft on the input side would increase to 20lb/ft at the output side.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: TLDR of my earlier comment is "search DAK engineering to find a simple to use gear calculator, there are several packages of free software, and it's I believe called ACE gear calc"

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: there is a spur gear calculator that I believe spits out a DXF file that will even convert (in corel for sure, but maybe inkscape for a free software that works with vector graphics) as just about anything like PDFs and such, but being DXF means its a path way, thus you can use it to make a shape in a 3D modeler that you just need to extrude.  It adds all sorts of functions too so you can control all the aspects of a spur gear system too. Where? I believe its name is ace and is on the DAK engineering site (no URL because youtube comments does not like this, but you can find it by searching and you will find it in the free software on that same site that also offers turboCNC the earliest free to use CNC software as far as I know, (but requires being ran in DOS so the software will have direct access to the motherboards timing hardware to ensure no lost steps and such) and even offers up to 8 axis of control something that even expensive programs like MACH3 didn't offer (I think the MACH brand of CNC control software only went up to 5 axis at most, and started at just 3 axis) of course now we have reprap which has up to 16 axis control and can be switched between CNC and 3D printer (ie between additive and subtractive) functions so... (basically it really only cares about the G-code and one way it starts at the base and works it's way down, while the other works it's way up depending on if its made with a subtractive slicer, or additive one, like cura, thus if you make the G-code in a slicer (cad-cam) for subtractive and run it in 3D printer it just seems the only issue is the software may be looking for bed heating and nozzle heating functions while not knowing what to do with fluid coolant and such codes I would guess. )

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: Hello Rob, you should check out "What makes magnetic gearboxes so amazing?" by Retsetman... (on youtube).... it will blow your mind. And, this type of gearbox offers a lot of advantages over the standard method... for one, it is very compact, so you save a lot of 3D printing filament (and thus printing time)... and for another, it has a built in "over strain" protection system... meaning that if something spins the generator too hard it will simply "let go" until it synchronizes down to something it can grab... Of course, that does mean that this type of gear will have more difficulty at higher forces because it will slip. Additionally, because it is magnetically driven, there will be a bit of "wiggle" in it, so it wouldn't be a great option where precision positioning is required.

author: @skampp
text: Great video Rob, thank you.

author: @manofausagain
text: Hey nice info. Pitch circle is what we use to generate a gear. Never hear of reference circle. If you are going to print gears, go for herringbone gears. Helical gears are quieter and transmit less vibration because of the more constant mesh. But they have axial load. Two gears back to back make a herringbone. Guess what sort of gears are in a destroyer gearbox? I'm not allowed to say but I'm pretty sure I gave a good hint!!

replies.author: @lesmaybury793
replies.text:
Pitch circle is associated with imperial gears. Also imperial gears use diametrical pitch (DP) instead of module.
When you see a gear referenced by module number you know you are going to be cutting a metric gear. Actually I find metric easier having been bought up in the imperial world.

replies.author: @manofausagain
replies.text: Ok. Well my understanding is that pitch circle diameter is what I'd used in the formulas I have used for all gears that are generated.

author: @silverpc4611
text: what happens if you glue a smaller gear to the the center of the bigger gear? and then add 3 to 4 of these things together.

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: I am not certain exactly what you are asking but I will try ... a smaller gear turning the outside of a larger gear will take a lot of rotations to move the outer gear 1 full circle... so you will gain torque. If you do that many times, you will multiply torque at the final stage.  However, the final stage will also spin horribly slowly.

replies.author: @silverpc4611
replies.text: ​@marcfruchtman9473 Compound gears and the Escapement Clock Mechanism is what i am thinking of. and using that to turn a generator.

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: ​@silverpc4611 None of what you first said mentioned an escapement mechanism. Why do you want to add that?

replies.author: @silverpc4611
replies.text: @marcfruchtman9473 consistent power over time. and the only work you have to do is whined it up. also maybe a flywheel could be added.

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: @silverpc4611 I see. So, a wind up device or flywheel would store the energy. Do you have a web link to an actual flywheel mechanism that incorporates an escapement mechanism? (would be cool to see that) ,also that seems like an interesting idea if you could get it working.

author: @mikekehler3078
text: Great videos , you make them easy for the ordinary joe to understand. Question for you if your drive gear has to turn 4 times to make the driven gear turn once I would assume they speed of each gear is different, the drive gear would be 4 times as fast as the driven gear?

replies.author: @howardosborne8647
replies.text: yes, in that scenario if the input drive gear is rotating at 100 rpm and the ratio between input and output gears is 4:1 then the driven gear is rotating at 400 rpm. Think about how the relationship between the chain sprockets on a pushbike with gears works and you'll get a clear picture of the relationship between gear/sprocket size and revolutions per turn of the input gear.

author: @victoryfirst2878
text:
This is a field of study or should I say field of work that I am really familiar with Robert. I am a machinist tool die maker by trade. The gear is something that I have been cutting for years, bevel, spiral, harribone types were what I produced....
 My machine that I loved and setup was a SUNDERLAND GEAR PLANER. Such a simple unit with accuracy second to none. The largest diameter I cut was 8 feet with a width of a foot and a half. Great memories which will always be my fondest. 
Continue your great channel Sir and keep teaching us all that everyone is capable of greatness on a budget. Drop a line if you need any directional guidance too. Peace vf
